sections.heading: A finished-fabric range built around double-knit structure
paragraphs: The commercial range starts after knitting: dyed, finished, brushed, patterned, yarn-dyed or otherwise prepared fabrics intended for apparel sampling and production. This distinction matters because buyers evaluate hand, surface, color, usable width, GSM, stretch, recovery and garment behavior on the finished state.

The supplied workbook contains 104 product records across 11 documented finished-fabric series: air-layer, yarn-dyed wool-blend air-layer, structured polyester-viscose, faux-cashmere, acrylic-wool, lyocell-acetate-wool composite, acetate brushed, lyocell-acetate-wool texture, raised-pile, cashmere-blend and jacquard. The examples below preserve the article-level composition, usable width and GSM from that workbook.

sections.heading: Manufacturing evidence without overclaiming
paragraphs: The machine sheet records 63 double-knit/84-feed machines, 44 rib/72-feed machines, and 114 double-knit/72-feed machines. sections.heading: Terminology safeguards for international buyers
paragraphs: Use lyocell unless branded fibre documentation supports a trademarked name. Use cashmere-feel or faux cashmere when the hand is similar but the composition does not contain cashmere. Use air-layer or scuba-style double knit until a true spacer structure has been verified.

These distinctions reduce quotation errors and make the content more useful to AI search systems because claims remain bounded by evidence. Accurate vocabulary is a conversion tool: it helps the buyer request the right article and helps the supplier explain a legitimate alternative.

sections.heading: What interlock fabric means in a sourcing brief
paragraphs: Interlock uses two connected rib-like systems to create a fabric that commonly presents smooth faces, balanced appearance, and greater stability than many single knits. It is considered for tops, dresses, childrenswear, linings, uniforms, and other programs that need a clean surface with moderate structure.

For sourcing, the category name is only the starting point. Buyers should record the exact construction, fibre composition, target GSM, usable width, surface or brushing treatment, color reference, stretch direction, recovery expectation, and intended garment. Do not treat interlock, Ponte Roma, and generic double knit as interchangeable. They can share a double-knit family while differing in yarn, loop arrangement, elastane, density, weight, surface, and recovery.

sections.heading: Where interlock can fit an apparel range
paragraphs: Interlock is often shortlisted for polished tops, dresses, childrenswear, uniforms, soft structure, linings, and selected sports or loungewear pieces. The correct choice depends on silhouette, climate, layering, seam construction, care route, and the amount of body or recovery the designer needs. A visual match is not enough: sample the fabric in the intended garment or panel.

Prioritize surface clarity and stability for polished garments; prioritize stretch and recovery only when the fibre and construction support them. Compare candidate swatches under the same light and condition, then document why one version was selected. It also differs from printed fabric because the motif is formed during knitting rather than added only as a surface image.

opening: Double knit fabric is produced with two needle systems, creating interconnected loop structures that often give more body, stability, and surface balance than single jersey. The family includes interlock, Ponte Roma, air-layer, rib-derived, and other double-sided constructions. Buyers should use the family name to shortlist options, then approve the exact construction, composition, GSM, width, finish, recovery, and garment result.

hero.alt: Finished navy double knit and teal interlock fabric swatches shown face and reverse for comparison
hero.caption: Double knit is a broad structural family; the approved article must define its specific construction and finished behavior.

sections.heading: Double knit describes a family, not one fixed fabric
paragraphs: A double-knit machine forms linked structures using two needle beds or cylinders. The resulting fabric can have two usable-looking faces, increased thickness, reduced edge curl, or greater shape stability, although each result depends on the actual structure and yarn setup.

Interlock tends toward smooth balanced faces; Ponte Roma tends toward compact body and controlled give; air-layer structures may create cushioning; jacquard can build pattern into the knit; and rib configurations create vertical texture and extension. These labels help organize sourcing, but they are not substitutes for the article specification.

note.label: Direct buyer answer
note.text: Use double knit when the program needs more structure or stability than a typical lightweight single knit, then choose the exact subtype by garment function and sample evidence.

sections.heading: Double knit versus single jersey in buyer decisions
paragraphs: The common distinction is structural. Single jersey usually has visibly different face and reverse sides and may curl at cut edges, while many double knits are more balanced and substantial. That difference can affect drape, opacity, seam profile, consumption, and the amount of shape a garment holds.

Neither family is automatically higher quality. A light T-shirt may benefit from jersey drape, while a fitted dress or structured jacket may benefit from double-knit body. Compare construction and finish under the real pattern rather than ranking fabrics by thickness alone.

sections.heading: Where finished double knit fabric fits
paragraphs: Double knit can support dresses, skirts, trousers, jackets, uniforms, hoodies, sports-inspired pieces, winter layers, loungewear, and statement jacquards. The broad range is why the garment application should appear at the top of the inquiry.

A buyer seeking clean tailoring may prioritize compactness and recovery; a hoodie may prioritize cushioning and seam comfort; a winter layer may prioritize warmth and brushed hand; a patterned piece may prioritize motif scale and reverse appearance.

For a broad request, ask the supplier to propose two or three constructions and explain the trade-off in body, recovery, surface, care and price. If a requirement is not yet known, mark it as a proposal field instead of leaving it implicit. That allows the supplier to explain the trade-off and prevents an exploratory sample from being mistaken for final approval.

sections.heading: How interlock construction creates balanced surfaces
paragraphs: Interlock forms connected loops in a way that can produce similar-looking face and reverse sides. Compared with many single jerseys, it often feels fuller, lies flatter at a cut edge, and provides a cleaner base for selected garments or decoration routes.

The exact result changes with yarn count, gauge, loop length, fibre, elastane, density, dyeing and finishing. A smooth double-sided sample may also be another double-knit structure, so a supplier should name the construction on the article sheet rather than rely only on visual description.

sections.heading: Interlock, single jersey and Ponte are not interchangeable
paragraphs: Single jersey often provides lighter drape and visibly different faces. Interlock commonly provides smooth balanced faces and moderate body. Ponte Roma typically provides more compact structure and tailoring body, although commercial naming can blur these boundaries.

Choose by garment behavior. A light tee may call for jersey, a polished soft layer may call for interlock, and fitted tailoring may call for Ponte. If two constructions appear plausible, request both and run the same pattern and care checks.

opening: Ponte fabric, often called Ponte Roma or Ponte di Roma, is a structured double knit used for garments that need body, a clean surface, controlled give, and shape retention. It is common in dresses, skirts, trousers, jackets, uniforms, and fitted workwear. Composition and recovery vary widely, so buyers must approve the exact article rather than assume one universal Ponte specification.

hero.alt: Finished black, burgundy, cobalt and grey Ponte fabric swatches beside tailored pattern pieces
hero.caption: Ponte selection connects body and recovery to the pattern, seam, care route and approved finished-fabric sample.

sections.heading: Ponte Roma is a structured double-knit category
paragraphs: Ponte is recognized for compact body and a relatively smooth profile. It can hold a garment line more firmly than many jersey fabrics, while selected constructions provide enough give for pull-on or fitted silhouettes.

Commercial terms Ponte, Ponte Roma, Ponte de Roma and Ponte di Roma may describe related fabrics, but they do not guarantee one composition or stretch level. Polyester, viscose, nylon, wool and elastane can appear in different blends. The article sheet and sample govern the sourcing decision.

note.label: Direct buyer answer
note.text: Use Ponte for structured knit tailoring, but choose the article by composition, GSM, compactness, stretch, recovery and seam behavior rather than by the Ponte name alone.

sections.heading: Ponte versus interlock and scuba-style fabric
paragraphs: Interlock often provides smoother balanced softness, Ponte often provides compact tailoring body, and scuba-style fabric often provides more cushioning or thickness. These are practical tendencies rather than universal rules.

If the garment needs a sharp trouser or dress line, start with Ponte. If it needs a softer layer, compare interlock. If it needs a cushioned modern silhouette, compare air-layer or scuba-style articles. Run the same pattern panel across options.

sections.heading: Ponte supports structured knit tailoring
paragraphs: Common programs include dresses, skirts, trousers, leggings with substantial body, jackets, uniforms, travelwear, workwear, and coordinated sets.
The same fabric may work differently across patterns. A fitted trouser stresses recovery and knee growth; a dress stresses drape and seam; a jacket stresses pressing, bulk and edge definition.

sections.heading: Specify recovery and compactness, not only weight
paragraphs: Record composition, construction, GSM, width, thickness, stretch direction, recovery, surface, luster, color, finish and garment application. Do not collapse these fields into a single adjective such as premium, soft, heavy, or stretchy. The supplier and buyer need the same written target and the same labeled physical reference.

A heavier GSM does not automatically mean better recovery or cleaner tailoring. Compare extension and return after the movement expected in the garment. If a requirement is not yet known, mark it as a proposal field instead of leaving it implicit. That allows the supplier to explain the trade-off and prevents an exploratory sample from being mistaken for final approval.

Sew the intended seam and test a high-movement panel such as knee, elbow, seat or waist where relevant. Review pressing and care because heat or moisture can alter the finished appearance.

faq.q: Is Ponte fabric the same as Ponte Roma?
faq.a: The terms are often used for related structured double knits. Commercial naming varies, so the approved construction, composition and sample matter more than spelling.

faq.q: Does Ponte fabric stretch?
faq.a: Many Ponte fabrics provide give, and some include elastane, but direction and recovery vary. Request article-specific stretch and recovery information.

faq.q: Is Ponte fabric suitable for trousers?
faq.a: It is a common starting category for pull-on and tailored knit trousers when the article provides appropriate body, recovery, opacity, seam and care performance.

faq.q: Is Ponte fabric suitable for summer?
faq.a: It depends on weight, fibre, construction, climate and garment. Many Ponte articles are substantial, so evaluate thermal comfort and layering for the target market.

faq.q: What should a Ponte sample test include?
faq.a: Include structure, GSM, width, stretch, recovery, growth, pilling plan, seam, pressing, opacity, color and care checks relevant to the garment.

opening: Scuba knit fabric is a structured, often smooth double knit used for apparel that needs body, cushioning, shape, and a modern clean surface. It is a textile fabric and should not be confused automatically with foam-backed neoprene. Air-layer is a related commercial category, while true spacer fabric requires a verified three-dimensional connecting structure. Buyers should confirm the exact article on a finished sample.

hero.alt: Structured black, cobalt, burgundy and grey scuba knit swatches arranged for apparel development
hero.caption: Scuba and air-layer names require structure confirmation before a buyer decides on garment, seam, heat and care performance.

sections.heading: Scuba knit is a textile double knit, not automatic neoprene
paragraphs: Apparel scuba knit typically uses a dense knitted structure to create a smooth surface and cushioned body. It may support clean silhouettes, resist some edge curl, and feel more substantial than jersey.

Neoprene usually refers to a synthetic rubber foam system, while scuba knit can be an entirely textile construction. Air-layer fabrics may create volume between surfaces, but the phrase does not prove a true spacer architecture. Use each term only after construction review.

note.label: Direct buyer answer
note.text: Scuba knit is a structured textile double knit. Use it for body and shape, but confirm whether the offered article is scuba-style, air-layer, or true spacer before specifying performance.

sections.heading: Scuba knit, air-layer, spacer and neoprene
paragraphs: These terms overlap in marketing but describe different technical possibilities. Scuba-style fabric points to smooth structured knit body; air-layer points to volume between knitted surfaces; spacer points to a verified three-dimensional structure; neoprene points to foam-based material.

The buyer should ask for the construction description and inspect the cut edge. If breathability, compression, insulation or technical spacing is critical, a commercial name is insufficient and a test plan is needed.

rows: Same fibre or care as textile knit

sections.heading: Match structure to silhouette and heat management
paragraphs: Scuba-style and air-layer fabrics may suit hoodies, jackets, dresses, skirts, structured trousers, sports-inspired casualwear, loungewear, bags or panels.
A dramatic silhouette may benefit from body, but the same thickness can increase seam bulk and thermal load. Evaluate comfort, movement, pressing and needle response in the final garment environment.

If a requirement is not yet known, mark it as a proposal field instead of leaving it implicit. That allows the supplier to explain the trade-off and prevents an exploratory sample from being mistaken for final approval.

faq.a: No. Scuba knit is generally a textile double knit, while neoprene commonly refers to synthetic rubber foam material. Confirm the exact construction.

faq.q: Is air-layer fabric a spacer fabric?
faq.a: Not automatically. True spacer fabric has a verified three-dimensional connecting structure. Air-layer may describe other cushioned double knits.

opening: Rib knit fabric uses alternating knit and purl columns to form visible vertical ribs and useful crosswise extension. The family includes fine 1x1 ribs, wider 2x2 ribs, compact trim ribs, and fashion ribs used as body fabric. Stretch and recovery are not fixed by the name, so buyers must approve the exact gauge, composition, weight, width, finish, and garment behavior.

hero.alt: Finished navy rib knit fabric beside dyed double-knit swatches and sample inspection tools
hero.caption: Rib approval connects rib repeat, gauge and recovery to either a trim function or a full garment panel.

sections.heading: Rib structure creates columns and crosswise extension
paragraphs: Alternating knit and purl wales create the characteristic ridged surface. The structure can draw inward and extend across the width, making rib useful where a garment needs close fit, recovery or a visible linear texture.

Rib repeat, machine gauge, yarn, elastane, density and finishing change the outcome. A fine body rib for a dress and a compact collar rib for a sweatshirt may share a family name while behaving very differently in width, recovery, weight and seam bulk.

note.label: Direct buyer answer
note.text: Rib is a vertical-textured knit family. Choose the exact repeat and recovery by whether the fabric will act as trim, body fabric or a fashion panel.

sections.heading: Body rib and trim rib require different approval logic
paragraphs: Trim rib must coordinate with a body fabric and recover after extension at collars, cuffs or waistbands. Body rib must also satisfy opacity, drape, growth, comfort and dimensional requirements across larger garment panels.

Do not approve trim from a loose strip alone. Attach it to the body fabric using the planned seam and ratio. For body rib, use a full panel or garment because widthwise draw-in and growth affect pattern consumption and fit.

sections.heading: Rib spans trims, fitted apparel and fashion texture
paragraphs: Rib is used in collars, cuffs, neckbands, waistbands, tanks, fitted tops, dresses, skirts, loungewear, base layers and contrast panels.

A visible wide rib can become the design, while a fine rib can read as a smooth fitted surface. The buyer should state rib repeat and visual scale rather than leave the supplier to interpret a reference photo.

Extend the fabric only to the range expected in the garment and observe recovery after an agreed interval. Excessive test extension can create misleading failure that the garment would never experience.

faq.q: What is the difference between 1x1 and 2x2 rib?
faq.a: The terms describe arrangements of knit and purl columns. They create different visual scales, draw-in, thickness and recovery, which must be checked on the actual article.

faq.q: Is rib knit only used for cuffs?
faq.a: No. Rib is used for trims and also for tanks, fitted tops, dresses, skirts, base layers, loungewear and fashion panels.

faq.q: Does rib knit need spandex?
faq.a: Not always. Rib structure can stretch without elastane, while elastane may be added for particular extension and recovery. Confirm composition and performance.

faq.q: Why does rib fabric look narrower when relaxed?
faq.a: Rib structures can draw inward because of their loop arrangement. Handling, condition, density and fibre also affect relaxed width.

faq.q: How should trim rib be approved?
faq.a: Sew it to the actual body fabric at the planned ratio, then check appearance, recovery, seam bulk, color match and care response.

opening: Jacquard knit and woven jacquard both build pattern into the textile structure, but they use different yarn systems and behave differently in stretch, drape, edge stability, reverse appearance, seam construction, and garment fit. Knit jacquard can support flexible apparel and structured double-knit effects, while woven jacquard can support crisp tailoring or decorative stability. Buyers should compare the exact sample, not the pattern image alone.

hero.alt: Finished green, aubergine and gold jacquard knit swatches with geometric patterns and yarn cones
hero.caption: Jacquard selection combines pattern decisions with structure, repeat, reverse, stretch, seam and cutting yield.

sections.heading: Jacquard describes pattern control, not one fabric behavior
paragraphs: Jacquard technology controls selected yarns or loops to build a motif as part of the textile. In knitting, loops create the structure; in weaving, warp and weft interlace. The visual idea can be similar while the mechanical behavior is different.

Knit jacquard may provide extension, softness or double-knit body. Woven jacquard may provide dimensional stability, crispness or complex interlacing. Fibre, density, yarn and finishing can reverse some broad tendencies, so the buyer must evaluate the offered article.

note.label: Direct buyer answer
note.text: Choose knit jacquard when loop-based stretch or knit body supports the garment; choose woven jacquard when woven stability or crisp structure is the better fit. Approve repeat and reverse in both cases.

sections.heading: Structure changes stretch, drape and seam planning
paragraphs: A knit jacquard can move with a fitted or flexible garment, while a woven jacquard may hold a more architectural line. Knit edges, loop snagging and recovery differ from woven fraying, seam allowance and pressing behavior.

Pattern scale also interacts with structure. The same motif can distort differently under knit extension or across woven shaping. Ask for a full repeat and cut a representative pattern panel before committing to garment placement.

paragraphs: Jacquard knit can suit dresses, cardigans, jackets, tops, skirts, coordinated sets and panels. Woven jacquard can suit jackets, skirts, dresses, trousers, occasionwear, accessories and decorative sections.
Map the motif to the pattern pieces, grading range and seam locations. A beautiful repeat can create unacceptable waste or awkward joins if scale and placement are ignored until cutting.

faq.q: Is jacquard a print?
faq.a: Jacquard patterns are generally built through yarn and structure control rather than only printed on the surface. A finished fabric may combine techniques, so confirm the process.

faq.q: Does jacquard knit stretch?
faq.a: Many knit jacquards provide some extension, but direction, amount and recovery vary with construction, fibre, elastane, density and finishing.

faq.q: Which is better for jackets?
faq.a: Either can work. Knit jacquard may support flexibility and knit body; woven jacquard may support crisp structure. Choose by pattern, silhouette, seam, lining and care.

faq.q: Why does the reverse matter?
faq.a: Floats, contrast yarns and alternate surfaces can affect snagging, lining, comfort, seam, cutting and appearance at hems or openings.

faq.q: What should a jacquard sample include?
faq.a: Request at least one full repeat with article data, repeat dimensions, colors, face and reverse identification, GSM, width, finish, stretch and relevant test plan.

opening: Air-layer knit fabric should be sourced by article evidence, not by the category name alone. Start with the required garment structure, then compare composition, GSM, usable width, recovery and finished-sample behaviour before requesting current commercial confirmation.

evidenceSnapshot.heading: Read three air-layer articles as different sourcing decisions

GD2515, GD2518 and GD2672 share a 160 cm recorded usable-width direction, yet their composition and weight records lead to different questions about body, drape, recovery, colour construction and care. Use these entries to shortlist samples, not to infer current stock or final performance.

sections.heading: Start with the garment result, not the air-layer label
paragraphs: Air-layer is a commercial family name used for structured finished knits. It does not prove one universal loop arrangement, thickness or performance level. A buyer should define the garment, silhouette, season, target body, stretch direction, recovery, surface, care route and sewing method before choosing an article.

The catalogue provides several evidence points. GD2515 is recorded at 300 GSM and 160 cm with 45% polyester, 25% combed cotton, 24% modal and 6% elastane. GD2518 is a lighter 260 GSM direction at 160 cm. These records show real variety inside the category; they do not make the articles interchangeable.

paragraphs: Use composition, GSM and usable width to narrow the catalogue, then inspect the complete finished sample. A 260 GSM air-layer and a 300 GSM air-layer may differ in body, compression, drape, opacity and sewing response even when both are described with the same market term.

GD2672 is a yarn-dyed wool-blend air-layer article recorded at 360 GSM and 160 cm. Its composition includes polyester, viscose, nylon, acrylic, elastane and wool. This is a distinct direction from cotton, modal or lyocell blends and should be evaluated for colour construction, hand, pilling, care and garment use on its own evidence.

Record the method and result instead of relying on adjectives such as premium or stable.

paragraphs: Send the article reference or closest benchmark, garment type, composition direction, target GSM, usable width, colour, finish, stretch and recovery needs, testing, quantity and destination. Add clear images of the face, reverse and garment if a physical swatch cannot be sent first.

Ask the supplier to separate article data from current commercial confirmation. The response should identify the proposed article, available sample route and any requested alternatives. Live availability, colour, quantity, price, MOQ, lead time, packing, documentation and shipping are confirmed for the current inquiry.

note.label: Decision rule
note.text: Use catalogue data to shortlist. Use a labeled finished sample to approve performance. Use the current quotation to confirm commercial conditions.

faq.q: Is every air-layer knit a spacer fabric?
faq.a: No. Air-layer is a commercial category name and does not by itself prove a spacer construction. Confirm the exact loop or layered structure, face, reverse, thickness and finished behaviour for the proposed article.

faq.q: Which GSM should an air-layer buyer choose?
faq.a: Choose GSM with the garment silhouette, season, opacity, body and drape. The catalogue includes examples from 260 GSM to heavier directions, but the physical sample and garment trial should decide suitability.

faq.q: What does usable width mean in an RFQ?
faq.a: Usable width is the cuttable width agreed for planning consumption, excluding edges that cannot be used. Confirm the measurement method and production tolerance for the exact finished article.

faq.q: Does a catalogue article guarantee stock?
faq.a: No. A catalogue record documents a sourcing direction. Live colour, quantity, sample availability and commercial terms require confirmation when the buyer sends the current inquiry.

evidenceSnapshot.heading: Use composition and article identity before judging softness
evidenceSnapshot.summary: Three catalogue references show why wool-blend sourcing cannot stop at a tactile impression. GD2672, 2552-240g and GD2579 combine different structures, recorded widths and surface questions even though each contains wool. The article sheet establishes the comparison starting point; the labelled cutting, garment trial and current quotation establish what can be approved and supplied.

items.detail: Recorded at 240 GSM and 160-165 cm, this article adds motif repeat, reverse appearance, snagging and placement decisions to the normal wool-blend sample review.

sections.heading: Read the full composition instead of the blend name
paragraphs: Wool may be one component in a multi-fibre construction designed around hand, structure, colour and cost. Ask for every fibre percentage and keep the article number attached to that declaration. Do not infer fibre content from a soft surface or a commercial name such as cashmere-feel.

The catalogue demonstrates the range. GD2672 contains 1.7% wool within a yarn-dyed air-layer blend and is recorded at 360 GSM and 160 cm. Acrylic-wool article 2552-240g contains 3.5% wool and is recorded at 240 GSM with a 170-175 cm width reference. They serve different sourcing briefs.
bullets: Record the complete fibre breakdown and article number.
State whether the priority is warmth, hand, structure, drape or appearance.
Confirm whether the surface is brushed, raised, compact or otherwise finished.
Match care and testing requirements to the destination market and garment label.

sections.heading: Use article data to compare weight and width
paragraphs: GSM helps describe material mass, while usable width affects consumption and pattern planning. Neither proves softness, warmth or quality alone. Compare the recorded values with a full-width or garment-relevant sample, then confirm the tolerances and method in the production specification.

The catalogue includes wool-related directions at different weights and widths. GD2672 is 360 GSM at 160 cm; 2552-240g is 240 GSM at 170-175 cm; GD2579 is a wool-blend jacquard at 240 GSM and 160-165 cm. The buyer should shortlist by garment need rather than by fibre label alone.

sections.heading: Test the surface and care route on the exact sample
paragraphs: Inspect pilling, abrasion, shedding, snagging and colour transfer where relevant. Compare the face and reverse before and after the intended laundering, pressing or dry-cleaning route. If the surface is brushed, record pile direction and appearance changes after rubbing or repeated care.

A garment trial reveals seam bulk, needle response, edge behaviour, lining needs, shrinkage and shape retention. Keep the tested sample, results and approval note together. A pass on one article or finish does not automatically transfer to another composition or colour.
steps: Photograph and label the original sample.

sections.heading: Separate sample approval from commercial confirmation
paragraphs: The RFQ should include garment, season, article or benchmark, composition direction, GSM, usable width, colour, finish, quantity, destination, testing and documentation. Ask the supplier to identify any substitute and explain the changed fields rather than presenting it as equivalent.

Sample approval confirms the reviewed material. Commercial confirmation establishes live availability, colour, quantity, price, MOQ, lead time, packing, payment and shipping for the order being discussed. Keeping those decisions separate makes the sourcing record clearer.

note.label: Evidence rule
note.text: A soft hand is an observation. Fibre percentages, test results, approved samples and current quotations are separate forms of evidence.

faq.q: Does wool blend fabric always feel warm?
faq.a: No. Warmth depends on construction, weight, density, surface, air retention, lining and garment design as well as wool percentage. Approve the exact finished sample for the intended season.

faq.q: Can buyers rely on the word cashmere-feel?
faq.a: No. Cashmere-feel describes a surface impression, not fibre content. Ask for the full composition declaration and do not infer cashmere unless the documented article data states it.

faq.q: What tests matter for brushed wool blends?
faq.a: Common checks include pilling, abrasion, shedding, colourfastness, dimensional stability and appearance after the intended care route. The buyer should specify applicable methods and acceptance criteria.

faq.q: Should width be quoted as full or usable width?
faq.a: Use usable width for consumption planning and state the agreed measurement method. Confirm production tolerance on the exact finished article before final marker planning.

opening: Jacquard knit fabric weight and width should be read with construction, repeat, face and reverse, stretch, finish and intended garment placement. GSM and usable width guide body and consumption, but the approved sample determines whether the pattern and performance fit the program.

evidenceSnapshot.heading: Translate jacquard GSM and width into marker decisions
evidenceSnapshot.summary: The catalogue records GD2579, GD2683 and GD2583 within a 160-165 cm usable-width direction, while their weight and article identity remain distinct. Those figures help a buyer estimate body and planning assumptions, but they do not establish usable yield by themselves. Repeat size, reverse construction, edge loss, motif placement and the approved finished width still need to be measured.

items.label: GD2579: 240 GSM reference
items.detail: Use the lighter recorded weight to review drape, repeat scale and reverse appearance, then measure the approved usable width before final marker planning.

items.label: GD2683: 280 GSM reference
items.detail: The higher recorded body changes seam bulk and garment structure. Compare it with the same motif, width and sample-conditioning assumptions used for the lighter option.

items.label: GD2583: identity check before calculation

sections.heading: Use GSM to describe body, not quality
paragraphs: GSM records fabric mass per square metre. It helps buyers compare body and consumption, but it does not prove durability, warmth, softness or suitability by itself. Two jacquards at similar GSM can differ because of yarn, structure, repeat, density, finish and reverse floats.

The catalogue records GD2579 at 240 GSM and GD2683 at 280 GSM. Both are wool-blend jacquard directions with 160-165 cm width references, yet they should be reviewed as separate articles. Compare body, drape, pattern clarity, reverse, stretch and garment response on labeled samples.

sections.heading: Use usable width for marker and repeat planning
paragraphs: Usable width is the cuttable area available for pattern planning. A jacquard buyer also needs repeat dimensions and the orientation of the motif. The nominal roll width is not enough when edges, distortion, motif placement or shade zones reduce usable area.

Request enough material to see at least one complete repeat across the relevant direction. Place real garment pieces on the sample and estimate matching waste. Record whether motifs require centred placement, pair matching or one-way cutting.
bullets: Confirm usable width and measurement method.
Record horizontal and vertical repeat dimensions.

Estimate matching and placement waste before quoting consumption.
Check whether the design has a one-way cutting direction.

sections.heading: Inspect the sample beyond weight and width
paragraphs: Check composition, stretch direction, recovery, snagging, abrasion, seam appearance, pressing response, colourfastness and dimensional stability. Inspect the reverse because floats and contrast yarns can affect lining, skin contact, cutting, seams and durability.

Photographs help communicate pattern and colour, but they cannot replace tactile and dimensional review. Approve one labeled sample with article, colour, date and version, then connect the production test plan to that reference.
steps: Verify article number and complete composition.
Measure GSM, usable width and repeat by the agreed method.

Record sample approval and open commercial conditions.

sections.heading: Put the right fields in the jacquard RFQ
paragraphs: State garment, panel placement, artwork rights, article or reference, composition direction, GSM, usable width, repeat, colour, face, reverse, stretch, finish, quantity, destination, testing and delivery need. Mark proposal fields clearly so the supplier knows where alternatives are acceptable.

The current quotation should confirm availability, sample route, exact specification, price, MOQ, lead time, packing, documentation and shipping. Catalogue values are a factual starting point, not a substitute for commercial confirmation.

note.label: Planning rule
note.text: GSM describes mass. Usable width supports consumption. Repeat and placement determine how much of that width a garment can actually use.

faq.q: Is a heavier jacquard always better?
faq.a: No. Higher GSM generally adds body or mass, but suitability depends on silhouette, drape, repeat, yarn, finish, seams, care and season. Compare approved samples in the intended garment.

faq.q: Why is usable width important for jacquard?
faq.a: Usable width affects marker efficiency, especially when motifs need matching or centred placement. Confirm the cuttable width and repeat before final consumption planning.

faq.q: How much sample is needed for a jacquard review?
faq.a: Request enough to see at least one complete repeat and to place garment pattern pieces. A small swatch may hide scale, reverse behaviour and matching waste.

faq.q: Do GD2579 and GD2683 have the same specification?
faq.a: No. GD2579 is recorded at 240 GSM and GD2683 at 280 GSM, although both show 160-165 cm width references. Confirm all article details and samples separately.

opening: Brushed knit fabric finishes change hand, appearance, bulk and care behaviour without proving fibre content or durability. Buyers should identify the base construction and article, inspect face and reverse, then verify shedding, pilling, GSM, usable width, colour and care on the finished sample.

evidenceSnapshot.heading: Separate the base article from the raised-surface finish
evidenceSnapshot.summary: The catalogue includes acetate-brushed, raised-pile and faux-cashmere directions, but those labels describe different evidence and approval questions. GD2706, GD2650, GD2652 and GD2502 cannot be compared by softness alone. A buyer needs the full composition, face and reverse, pile direction, recorded weight and width, then must test shedding, pilling, seams and care on the exact finished sample.

items.label: GD2706: acetate-brushed direction
items.detail: The catalogue records 250 GSM. Confirm usable width, full composition, face and reverse, brushing consistency and care response before approval.

items.label: GD2650 and GD2652: raised-pile comparison

sections.heading: Separate base construction from surface finish
paragraphs: Brushing, raising and pile development alter the surface of a knit, while the underlying construction and composition still control stretch, recovery, stability and sewing response. A soft hand does not identify the fibre blend. Ask for the article number, full composition and finish description together.

The catalogue contains faux-cashmere, acetate brushed and raised-pile series. These labels describe sourcing directions, not a universal performance claim. GD2706 is an acetate-brushed article recorded at 250 GSM; GD2650 is a raised-pile article recorded at 240 GSM and 165 cm.

sections.heading: Compare documented finish directions
paragraphs: A faux-cashmere direction can produce a soft impression without containing cashmere. A cashmere-blend article should state cashmere in the actual composition record. The buyer must preserve this distinction in product copy, care labels and sourcing decisions.

Raised-pile GD2650 is recorded at 240 GSM and 165 cm. GD2652 is recorded at 250-260 GSM and 165 cm. Acetate-brushed entries may require usable width confirmation. These differences affect RFQ completeness and sample planning.

sections.heading: Test pilling, shedding and appearance after care
paragraphs: Inspect loose fibre, pile direction, streaks, shade, crush marks and surface consistency before testing. Run the buyer-required pilling, abrasion, shedding, colourfastness and dimensional methods, then compare appearance after the intended wash, dry, press or dry-clean route.

A garment trial should include seam and edge areas because surface and bulk may change under stitching or pressing. Record any appearance difference between panels cut in opposite directions. If pile direction matters, add a one-way cutting note to the garment specification.

sections.heading: Create a finish-specific sample and RFQ record
paragraphs: State base construction, exact composition, article or benchmark, GSM, usable width, face and reverse finish, pile direction, colour, garment, quantity, destination, testing and care requirements. Include expected surface appearance after laundering or wear.

Ask sales to confirm the proposed article, sample route and any alternative. Sample approval covers the reviewed physical reference. Current availability, colour, quantity, price, MOQ, lead time, packing, documentation and shipping require commercial confirmation.

note.label: Claim boundary
note.text: Words such as cashmere-feel describe an impression. Only the article composition record can support a fibre-content statement.

faq.q: Is brushed knit fabric the same as fleece?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Brushing is a surface process, while fleece is a broad market term often associated with a raised back. Confirm the base construction, treated side and finished sample.

faq.q: Does cashmere-feel mean the fabric contains cashmere?
faq.a: No. Cashmere-feel describes hand or surface. A cashmere content claim requires the exact article composition to state cashmere and any buyer-required verification.

faq.q: What should buyers check on raised-pile knit?
faq.a: Check pile direction, shedding, pilling, abrasion, colourfastness, surface consistency, GSM, usable width, seams and appearance after the intended care route.

faq.q: Why can usable width be missing from a finish record?
faq.a: Some catalogue records require width confirmation. Ask sales to measure the offered finished article and state the method and tolerance before consumption planning.

opening: A useful knit fabric RFQ identifies the garment, construction or article, complete composition, target GSM, usable width, colour, finish, performance tests, quantity and destination. It also separates sample approval from the commercial conditions that require a current quotation.

evidenceSnapshot.heading: Build one RFQ record that can survive sample revisions
evidenceSnapshot.summary: An actionable knit-fabric RFQ distinguishes facts that identify the requested article from fields that remain open for supplier confirmation. It also gives every sample revision a traceable decision record. This prevents a catalogue name, old message or reference image from becoming an accidental production specification and lets technical, commercial and logistics questions be answered in the correct order.

items.label: Measurable approval fields

items.label: Current commercial confirmation

sections.heading: Begin with the garment and decision context
paragraphs: Name the garment, target market, season, silhouette and panels where the fabric will be used. Attach a reference garment, swatch, technical sketch or article number when available. Explain which properties are fixed and where the supplier may propose an alternative.

A category name such as air-layer, Ponte, rib or jacquard is not a full specification. Add the required structure, face and reverse, stretch direction, recovery, opacity, drape, surface and care route so the supplier can evaluate fit.

sections.heading: Record measurable fabric specifications
paragraphs: State complete composition, target GSM, usable width, colour, finish and required tolerances. Add repeat dimensions for jacquard, pile direction for raised surfaces, and stretch or recovery methods for elastic constructions. If a field is unknown, request a proposal instead of inventing a value.

Catalogue examples show why exact fields matter. GD2515 is 300 GSM and 160 cm; GD2579 is 240 GSM with a 160-165 cm width reference; GD2683 is 280 GSM with the same width range. Similar category names do not make these articles equivalent.

sections.heading: Define sample approval and testing
paragraphs: Ask for a labeled sample with supplier, article, colour, date and version. State the minimum sample size needed for repeat, width, garment panel, washing or laboratory checks. List the test methods and acceptance criteria that apply to the buyer's market and garment.

Record observations and measured results separately. A sample can be approved for hand and appearance while laboratory or bulk shade checks remain open. The approval note should identify what was accepted and what must still be confirmed.

sections.heading: Request commercial confirmation without guessing terms
paragraphs: Include estimated quantity, colour plan, destination, packaging, documentation, inspection and timing needs. Ask sales to respond with the current article availability, sample route, price basis, MOQ, lead time, payment, packing and shipping conditions.

Do not treat catalogue inclusion as a live-stock guarantee. The catalogue supports discovery and technical comparison. The current quotation is the record for commercial confirmation, and production should reference the same approved sample and specification.

note.label: Copyable RFQ line
note.text: Please quote the proposed article against the attached garment brief and confirm composition, GSM, usable width, colour, finish, testing, sample route, quantity conditions, packing, lead time and shipping basis.

faq.q: What is the minimum information for a knit fabric RFQ?
faq.a: Provide garment use, construction or article, composition direction, GSM, usable width, colour, finish, quantity and destination. Add testing, sample and timing needs when known.

faq.q: Should a buyer specify usable width or total width?
faq.a: Use usable width for consumption planning and state the measurement method. The supplier should confirm the finished production tolerance for the proposed article.

faq.q: Can a buyer request an alternative article?
faq.a: Yes. Mark proposal fields clearly and ask the supplier to identify every changed specification. Review the alternative as a new labeled sample rather than assuming equivalence.

faq.q: When are MOQ and lead time confirmed?
faq.a: MOQ and lead time are commercial conditions confirmed for the current article, colour, quantity and order context. They should come from the current quotation, not a generic content page.

opening: These short knit fabric sourcing questions help apparel buyers move from a broad category to a verifiable article, finished sample and current quotation. Each answer identifies what the website can document and what still requires sample approval or commercial confirmation.

evidenceSnapshot.heading: Ask questions in the order that removes sourcing uncertainty
evidenceSnapshot.summary: Short sourcing answers are useful when they point to the next piece of evidence rather than offering a universal promise. Begin by identifying the article and construction, then confirm measurable finished-fabric fields, then approve the sample, and only then close current commercial conditions. This sequence keeps website facts, sample observations and quotation terms from being mixed into one unsupported claim.

items.label: What identifies the fabric?

items.label: What must be measured or observed?

items.label: What changes with the inquiry date?
